output:
Describing the input grid: The input consists of a periodic mosaic where each row and column follows a fixed modular color cycle, but some positions are blanked out with 0.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has exactly the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reconstruct each missing cell independently using the global periodic rule: identify the repeating row cycle and repeating column cycle from the visible cells, then assign each 0 the value dictated by its row index and column index within that periodic lattice.

Describing the input grid: The input is a large rectangular carpet made of a repeating tiled motif, with some cells replaced by 0 as missing holes.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps the same overall grid size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Recover the carpet by extending the repeating tile pattern across the entire grid. Determine the smallest repeating block from the nonzero regions, then fill every 0 cell with the value that would appear there by continuing the same horizontal and vertical repetition.

Describing the input grid: The input contains pairs of same-colored cells positioned so that their relative arrangement suggests a line to be completed.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each color, find the two extreme cells of that color and draw the straight 8-directional connection between them using that color, then preserve any existing colored cells; the result is the union of the original cells and the connecting line.

Describing the input grid: The input contains sparse colored anchor points, each color forming its own independent pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each color, identify the set of its cells, compute the monotone path that connects them by moving one step down and/or one step left/right as needed to make a continuous descending diagonal chain, and fill that path with the same color so that each color becomes a connected diagonal sequence.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 cups of the same color, one in the upper half and one in the lower half, with the same shape mirrored vertically around the separating blank row.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each cup independently, identify the highest row containing colored cells and then “pour water” downward by copying the colored cells of each row to all cells between the leftmost and rightmost colored positions on that row, creating a filled-in version of the cup while keeping the cup’s outer footprint unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two disconnected 4x4 “cups” of the same color, one above and one below a horizontal separator row of zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: In each 4x4 cup, find the colored cells in the top half of the cup and fill every cell horizontally between the leftmost and rightmost colored cell on each occupied row, preserving the original rows and leaving other rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid containing four colored endpoint cells arranged as two pairs, with the rest mostly black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the two nonzero cells in each row that form a pair. If the two cells have the same color, fill every cell between them in that row with the same color; otherwise leave the row unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x10 grid with pairs of colored cells placed at the far left and far right of certain rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, compare the colors of the leftmost and rightmost nonzero cells. If they are the same, draw a solid horizontal line of that color across the whole row from the left cell to the right cell; if they differ, keep the row exactly as it is.

Describing the input grid: The input contains teal 8s arranged in pairs or singletons across rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the topmost row and bottommost row that contain teal 8s. In each such row, if there are two teal 8s, fill the cells between them with green. If there is only one teal 8 in a row, extend a vertical green line downward from that 8 to the next row containing a teal 8 in the same column, stopping before the lower 8.

Describing the input grid: The input contains teal 8s that act as endpoints of straight-line segments.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every pair of teal 8s that lie in the same row and are the leftmost and rightmost 8s in that row, fill the horizontal segment between them with green. For every pair of teal 8s that lie in the same column and are the topmost and bottommost 8s in that column, fill the vertical segment between them with green. Keep all teal 8s unchanged and leave all other cells empty.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid where the middle row acts as a separator and the top and bottom rows are compared by their internal consistency.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Mark a row gray (5) only when all three cells in that row match exactly; otherwise mark the row black (0). The transformation is applied independently to each row, preserving the original row positions.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid whose rows each contain either one repeated color or a mixed pair of colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if all three cells are the same color, turn that entire row gray (5); otherwise turn that entire row black (0).

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid with three horizontal bands, and each band should be tested for whether it is uniform.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each row, if the row contains at least two different colors, turn that row black (0); if the row is completely uniform, turn that row gray (5).

Describing the input grid: The input is a small grid containing one connected non-black pattern made of a single color, positioned somewhere in the grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Move the entire colored pattern down by exactly one row, keeping its shape, color, and column positions unchanged; cells that move outside the grid disappear and their old locations become black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 5x5 patterns separated by a horizontal divider row of 4s, and both patterns use the same foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 6x5.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the top and bottom 5x5 patterns cell by cell. Mark a cell green if exactly one of the two patterns has the foreground color there, and black if both have the same value. The output is the 6x5 cellwise difference grid formed by this comparison, using the first five rows from the comparison and a sixth row from the remaining unmatched part of the lower pattern.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with a single top-row pattern of colored cells and empty space below.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Generate the output by vertically repeating a 2-row motif derived from the first row: row 0 is the input’s first row, row 1 is the first row shifted one cell left with wraparound disabled and each original colored cell duplicated diagonally down-left; then repeat these two rows downward until the grid is filled.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single solid-colored vertical block in the first column, with all other cells black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Keep the original first column unchanged. Place a red 2 in the rightmost cell of the top row, then continue placing 2s one step down-left on each subsequent row, forming a descending diagonal. The bottom row is then filled entirely with yellow 4s.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single solid-colored vertical block in the first column, with all other cells black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Preserve the leftmost colored column as-is. Add a red anti-diagonal of 2s across the empty area, starting at the top-right corner and moving down-left by one cell per row. After drawing that diagonal, color the full bottom row yellow with 4s, leaving the leftmost bottom cell unchanged if it already belongs to the original column.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid containing a short colored motif at the top and blank space beneath it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Extend the pattern downward by taking the sequence of non-zero rows from the top and appending a vertically flipped copy of those rows at the bottom, creating a symmetric completed grid.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid where the top part contains a vertical sequence of colored rows, followed by a block of empty rows.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the non-empty rows from the top of the grid to the bottom in reverse order, preserving each row exactly, so the grid becomes vertically symmetric around its middle.

Describing the input grid: The input is a rectangular grid with a pattern in the upper rows and empty rows below it.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the non-empty portion of the grid across a horizontal axis through the center of the grid, placing the top colored rows into matching rows at the bottom while keeping the original top rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of separate monochrome blocks whose outlines define rectangular frames.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each connected colored block, compute the smallest axis-aligned rectangle containing it. If the block occupies the rectangle’s perimeter, fill the rectangle’s interior with 8; if the block is a solid rectangle, replace only the interior cells of that rectangle with 8. Do this independently for each object, preserving the original border color and background.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two colored rectangles, one in the upper half and one in the lower half, each made of a single color with empty interior space or solid fill.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each colored rectangle, find its bounding box and recolor every cell strictly inside that box to 8 if it is not already part of the rectangle’s border. The outermost cells of the rectangle remain their original color, and all other cells remain 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two or more colored rectangular regions separated by empty space, and each colored region may have a different size and shape.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify each nonzero rectangular region. For every such region, fill its interior cells (all cells not on the border) with color 8, while keeping the border cells unchanged. Leave all background cells as 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more small 3x3 shapes made of color 4, separated by empty space.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 3x3 shape, fill every empty cell inside its 3x3 bounding box with color 7, leaving all existing 4-cells unchanged. This completes each shape into a solid 3x3 square or a 3x3 pattern where 7 marks the missing cells.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 3x3 blocks of color 4, each block possibly missing some cells, with all other cells empty.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 3x3 block, compare its cells to the ideal full 3x3 square of color 4. Any position that is empty in the input but lies inside the block’s 3x3 extent is filled with color 7, while all original 4 cells remain as 4.

Describing the input grid: The input shows partial outlines of 3x3 cube faces made from color 4, each occupying a 3x3 region.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Complete each partial cube face by painting color 7 into every missing cell inside its 3x3 region. The result is a fully specified 3x3 square where 4 represents the given boundary/structure and 7 represents the inferred missing interior or edge cells.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly empty square grid with a single non-zero cell marking a source point.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the source color into every cell whose position lies on one of the two diagonals through the source point, extending outward in all four diagonal directions until the edge of the grid is reached; all other cells remain 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a mostly empty square grid with one colored cell that acts as a center point.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Draw an X-shape of the source color centered at the given cell by filling all cells on both diagonals that pass through that cell, including the cell itself, so the pattern expands symmetrically toward the four corners.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid containing one colored cell inside an otherwise empty field.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: From the single colored cell, expand the color outward along diagonal lines in all four corner directions, but only on cells that remain at the same diagonal distance from the source as one another, producing the full diagonal cross pattern across the grid.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing one or more repeated colored regions, and the key information is the number of different colors used.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Determine the number of unique colors in the input. If there are 2 unique colors, draw gray cells on the main diagonal. If there are 3 unique colors, draw gray cells on the anti-diagonal. If there is only 1 unique color, draw gray cells across the entire top row. All remaining cells are black.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid filled with exactly two different nonzero colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count how many distinct colors appear in the input. Then place gray cells (5) on the main diagonal if there are exactly 2 distinct colors, on the anti-diagonal if there are exactly 3 distinct colors, and on the top row if there is only 1 distinct color; all other cells become black (0).

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid whose colors encode a count through the diversity of colors present.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Let k be the number of distinct colors in the input. Create an output grid of black cells, then place gray cells in a fixed pattern determined by k: k=1 means fill the top row, k=2 means fill the main diagonal, and k=3 means fill the anti-diagonal.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of four equal-sized square blocks arranged in a 2x2 or 3x2 layout, separated by rows/columns of zeros.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is a compact grid whose dimensions equal the number of block rows by the number of block columns.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero block, determine whether its cells are mostly filled or mostly empty by comparing the number of colored cells to the number of zero cells in that block. Then build the output as a summary matrix where each position contains the original block color if the block is mostly filled, and 0 if the block is mostly empty.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x3 grid containing blue cells (1) on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Count the number of blue cells in the input, then create a centered red shape in the output whose filled cells equal that count: for 1 fill the top-left cell, for 2 fill the top row left-to-right, for 3 fill the top row, for 4 fill the top row plus the center cell, and for 5 or more continue filling outward in the same fixed centered order.

Describing the input grid: The input is an n×n grid with a single colored pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 2n×2n.
Describing how to transform the grid: Create four copies of the input and place them in a 2×2 arrangement, but rotate each copy around the center of the 2n×2n canvas: top-left is the original grid, top-right is rotated 90° clockwise, bottom-right is rotated 180°, and bottom-left is rotated 270° clockwise.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two 4x4 patterns separated by a divider row, where the top pattern is a blue mask and the bottom pattern is a red mask.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compute the XOR of the two 4x4 masks after aligning them directly on top of each other: output 3 in cells where exactly one mask has a colored cell, and output 0 where both masks match, whether both are empty or both are filled.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 binary grids, one above and one below a full row of 4s that acts as a separator; the top grid uses 1s and the bottom grid uses 2s.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the top 4x4 grid and the bottom 4x4 grid cell-by-cell, treating 1 and 2 as opposite colors that cancel when they disagree. For each position, if both grids have the same value (both 0 or both filled), output 0; if exactly one of them is filled, output 3.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two 4x4 grids separated by a row of 4s, and the lower grid is compared against the upper grid after shifting it into the same position.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is always a 4x4 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Superimpose the two 4x4 grids and keep only the cells where the top and bottom grids differ in occupancy. A cell becomes 3 when exactly one of the two grids has a nonzero value there, and becomes 0 when both are the same; the actual nonzero colors 1 and 2 are ignored except as “filled” versus “empty”.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid containing isolated colored cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps the same grid size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every colored cell, extend its color horizontally to the right until the edge of the grid. If the colored cell is not already in the last column, also place the same color in the last column for every row below the seed’s row, forming an L-shape that turns at the right edge.

Describing the input grid: The input is a grid with a few nonzero “seed” cells, each seed having its own color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero seed cell, draw a horizontal stroke to the right starting at that cell and continuing to the last column. Then, at the last column, continue the stroke downward from that row to the bottom of the grid, using the same color. Existing nonzero cells remain unchanged unless they are overwritten by the stroke.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a background color with one connected non-background shape made of several colored cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the tight bounding box around that non-background shape.
Describing how to transform the grid: Crop the smallest rectangle that contains every cell whose color is not the background color, and keep the cells inside it exactly as they appear, replacing the background color by 0 in the cropped result.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a single object embedded in a uniform background, and the object may include background-colored holes inside its bounding box.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output size equals the height and width of the object’s bounding box.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the minimal axis-aligned rectangle that encloses all cells different from the background, then output that rectangle with all background-colored cells inside it converted to 0 and all other cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one rectangular region of interest made of non-background cells, possibly with some background cells interspersed.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is exactly the width and height of that region’s bounding rectangle.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the rows and columns that contain at least one non-background cell, take their full span as the crop, and copy that subgrid to the output while mapping every background-colored cell within the crop to 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more rectangular blocks made of a single nonzero color, separated by black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each solid rectangular block, replace its uniform color with a fixed 4-color frame pattern depending on the block’s width and height: the four corners become blue, the border cells become yellow, and the interior cells become red, while black background stays black. If the block is only 1 cell thick in any dimension, only the corresponding available parts of the frame are drawn.

Describing the input grid: The input contains one or more solid rectangular regions of a single color on a black background, all rectangles aligned to the grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Each colored rectangle is filled with a concentric border pattern: the outermost layer becomes blue at the corners and yellow along the edges, and the remaining inner area becomes red. The pattern is applied independently to every rectangle, preserving its shape and location.

Describing the input grid: The input is composed of rectangular color blocks on a black background, where each block is a filled rectangle.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every filled rectangle by a tiled motif of size 2 colors around a red center: draw blue only on the four corner cells of the rectangle, yellow on all edge cells excluding corners, and red on all non-edge interior cells. The original block color is ignored, and empty black cells remain unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 7x7 grid with four 2x2 corner patterns, and the middle row/column act as spacers that separate the corners.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Build the output by taking the outer boundary of the four corner patterns as if they were the four visible corners of a cube: the top-left corner fills the upper-left of the 3x3, the top-right corner fills the upper-right, the bottom-left corner fills the lower-left, and the bottom-right corner fills the lower-right, while the center cell is colored only if any corner pattern contributes to the center position.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of several isolated square frames made of 5s, with hollow centers.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output remains the same size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect each square frame and use its side length to assign a fill color from an ordered mapping of square size to color. Fill the inside of each square completely with that color, while preserving the 5-colored outline. The mapping is based on relative size among the squares in the grid, so different square sizes receive different interior colors.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several separate 5-colored square frames of different sizes, each with a hollow interior.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify each closed square frame and measure its side length. Fill the hollow interior of each square with a new color determined by the square’s size: one size maps to teal, another size maps to orange, and the largest size maps to fuchsia. Keep the original 5-colored border unchanged.

Describing the input grid: Each row has a “label color” in column 0, and the rest of the row contains one or more copied templates made of nonzero cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same shape as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat every nonzero cell in columns 1 onward as a placeholder to be recolored by the row label: if the row’s first cell is 1, 2, 3, or 4, recolor all nonzero cells in that row to that same value; do not alter the 0 cells or the first column.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains several horizontal sections separated by a fully nonzero “marker” row; the leftmost column gives the target color for each row, and the rest of each row contains a pattern of foreground cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every row, replace each nonzero cell to the right of the leftmost column with the color found in that row’s leftmost cell, while keeping all 0 cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The grid is divided into blocks by the fully filled marker row, and each row begins with a color code in the first column; the remaining cells in that row form a mask.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the leftmost-column color across every nonzero cell in the same row, but only within the regions where the input already has nonzero entries; all background cells stay 0.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a square grid with several isolated nonzero points, all having the same color, and the rest are black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find every nonzero point and expand it into a 3x3 filled square of color 1, but only within the bounds of the grid. The original point acts as the center of the square, so cells up to one step away in each direction are filled.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a square grid with several isolated nonzero points, all having the same color, and the rest are black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero point, draw a 3x3 block of color 1 whose top-left corner is one cell up and one cell left from the point. If multiple blocks overlap, the overlapping cells stay color 1; all untouched cells stay black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a square grid with several isolated nonzero points, all having the same color, and the rest are black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Replace every nonzero point by a 3x3 filled square of color 1 centered on that point. If these squares overlap, keep them as color 1; all other cells remain black.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a sparse arrangement of colored cells on a black background.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every row, take each colored cell and paint that same color into the cells directly beneath it in the same column, but only until another colored cell is encountered below; the final result is the vertical union of all downward color rays from the original points.

Describing the input grid: The input is a small square grid containing a few nonzero colored “seed” cells, with the rest black.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero seed cell, copy its color straight downward within its own column to every cell below it until the bottom of the grid; if multiple seeds affect the same cell, the lower seed’s color takes precedence over any color coming from above.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid with several colored points placed in different rows and columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same height and width as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Starting from the top row and moving downward, whenever a colored cell appears in a column, fill that color into all cells below it in the same column, so each column becomes vertically extended from its highest colored point downward.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains a 2×2 green block plus several isolated colored points. These points act as endpoints that define row and column lines through the green block.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify the rows and columns that intersect the 2×2 green block. For each isolated colored point, draw a straight line of that point’s color along its row or column until it reaches the nearest row or column of the green block, but only in the direction that creates an intersection with the green block. If multiple points share the same row or column, their drawn segments may overlap. Preserve the green block.

Describing the input grid: The grid contains one 2×2 green block and several single colored points of other colors scattered in the same grid.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Find the row(s) occupied by the 2×2 green block and the column(s) occupied by the 2×2 green block. For every other colored point, fill the entire horizontal row segment from that point to the green block if the point is in a different row but same column-intersection pattern, and fill the entire vertical column segment from that point to the green block if the point is in a different column but same row-intersection pattern. Keep the original green block unchanged and leave all other cells as black.

Describing the input grid: The input shows a periodic pattern in the bottom three rows, where each 5-row column slice repeats after a constant horizontal shift, while the top rows stay all zero.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Detect the repeating period from the non-zero portion of the bottom three rows, then extend the pattern by copying the observed period rightward across the entire row region, leaving the zero rows unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x6 grid containing two 3x3 shapes separated by a vertical boundary between columns 3 and 4.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Take the left 3x3 half and the right 3x3 half, map all nonzero cells from both halves into a single 3x3 output by treating any occupied cell as 6, and leave cells that are empty in both halves as 0.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 3x6 grid split into a left 3x3 half and a right 3x3 half, with the left half using color 4 and the right half using color 3.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is a 3x3 grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the left 3x3 half and the right 3x3 half cell-by-cell; if either half has a nonzero cell at a position, write 6 in the output at that position, otherwise write 0.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of sparse colored “labels” on the top row and multiple uniform colored rectangles below, with empty space between them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Treat each rectangle as an object to be painted. Determine its row span and column span, then use the nearest top-row colored cell whose column intersects that rectangle to choose the replacement color. Fill the entire rectangle with that chosen color, leaving all non-rectangle cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 10x10 grid with a few isolated single colored cells in the top row and several 2x2, 3x3, or 4x4 blocks of one repeated color elsewhere, all separated by empty rows/columns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each nonzero block, find the single colored cell that lies in the same row band and column band as that block’s position relative to the top markers, and recolor the entire block with that marker’s color. In other words, each block is filled using the color of the top-row cell aligned with its row/column region.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a set of colored reference cells along the top row, and several rectangular blocks of a different color placed below them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Partition the grid into regions determined by the intersections of the block locations with the top-row reference columns. Every solid block is recolored according to the top-row color in the same column interval, while cells outside the blocks remain unchanged. The block color is thus replaced by the corresponding top marker color for that block’s column position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 10x9 grid split by a vertical line of 5s in the center column. The left half is a 10x4 pattern of one color, and the right half is a 10x4 pattern of another color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 10x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Fold the grid along the central column of 5s so the left 4-column half is kept as the output area. For each cell in the left half, compare it with the mirror cell from the right half across the fold. If the right-side mirrored cell is nonzero, copy that color into the output; otherwise keep the left-side cell’s color. If both are nonzero, the right-side color takes precedence.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a 10x9 grid with a vertical separator of 5s. The two 10x4 halves on either side of the separator are two layers of a paper fold.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 10x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Fold the right half onto the left half by mirror symmetry across the 5-column. For each position, overlay the mirrored right-half cell onto the left-half cell. Nonzero cells from the folded-over side fill empty cells on the left, but do not overwrite nonzero cells already present on the left. The separator column is removed in the result.

Describing the input grid: The input is a 10x9 grid divided by a central column of 5s into two 10x4 panels.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 10x4.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the right panel horizontally and use it as a mask to complete the left panel. The output is formed by taking the left panel and, at every cell where the mirrored right panel has a nonzero value, replacing the left cell with that mirrored value; cells where the mirrored right panel is zero remain unchanged. The fold line itself is discarded.

Describing the input grid: The input contains a top pattern, a separator row of 5s, and a bottom pattern of the same dimensions; the task is to merge the two patterns.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output keeps only one panel, with the same dimensions as either the top or bottom section.
Describing how to transform the grid: Remove the separator row, then overlay the bottom panel onto the top panel cell-by-cell; wherever either panel has a nonzero value, copy that color into the output. If both panels have nonzero values in the same position, keep the color from the top panel unless the top is black, in which case use the bottom color.

Describing the input grid: The input contains two equal-width panels of colored cells separated by a full horizontal divider row of 5s; the top panel is the pattern to be combined with the bottom panel.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output has the same width as the input, but only the height of one panel (the divider and bottom panel are removed).
Describing how to transform the grid: Split the grid at the row of 5s, take the top panel and the bottom panel, vertically align them so corresponding cells overlap, and form a new panel by taking the union of the nonzero cells: a cell is colored if it is colored in either panel, and remains black only if both panels are black.

Describing the input grid: The input is composed of two same-sized halves separated by a horizontal line of 5s, with colors appearing in corresponding positions across the two halves.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output is the height of one half and the full original width.
Describing how to transform the grid: Compare the top and bottom halves position by position and construct the output by taking the union of their occupied cells after aligning them vertically. For each cell, if either half has a colored cell, place that color in the output; if both halves are colored, preserve the color that is present in the lower half.

Describing the input grid: The input is a square grid containing one non-black color and black cells.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Rotate the entire grid 90 degrees counterclockwise.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a tall grid whose top half is all background, and whose bottom half contains a vertically symmetric pattern.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Copy the bottom half of the input grid into the top half by flipping it vertically, so the entire grid becomes symmetric across the horizontal midline.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of a tall grid with a blank upper region and a lower region that contains a pattern aligned to the right edge and symmetric in the vertical direction.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Reflect the nonblank lower half upward across the horizontal center line, replacing the blank upper half with that mirrored copy while leaving the lower half unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input is split into two equal halves, where one half is empty and the other half contains the complete design.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: Take the nonempty half of the grid and duplicate it into the empty half by vertical reflection, producing a grid where the top and bottom halves are mirror images of each other.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two 3x3 binary masks stacked vertically, separated into a top mask using 9 as the foreground color and a bottom mask using 1 as the foreground color.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: Overlay the two 3x3 masks by treating 9 and 1 as the same occupied cell. Then take the complement of the overlay: cells occupied in either mask become 0, and cells occupied in neither mask become 2.

Describing the input grid: The input consists of two 3x3 patterns stacked vertically, one above the other.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is 3x3.
Describing how to transform the grid: First compute the union of the filled cells from the top and bottom patterns. Then invert that union within the 3x3 area, turning every filled position into 0 and every empty position into 2.

Describing the input grid: The input contains multiple separated 2x2 objects, some monochrome and some bicolored.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid keeps the same dimensions as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For every 2x2 object, count the number of unique colors appearing in that object. Starting from the row just below the object, copy a 2x2 green placeholder downward for a number of rows equal to that count, aligned to the same 2x2 position.

Describing the input grid: The input contains several small colored 2x2 blocks placed in different columns, with each block using one or two nonzero colors.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid has the same size as the input grid.
Describing how to transform the grid: For each 2x2 colored block, determine how many distinct nonzero colors it contains. Then fill the rows immediately below that block downward for exactly that many rows, using a 2x2 green block in the same columns. Leave all original colored cells unchanged.

Describing the input grid: The input grid has several 2x2 colored patterns, each occupying a distinct column pair, with empty space below them.
Describing the size of the output grid: The output grid is the same size as the input.
Describing how to transform the grid: Identify each 2x2 pattern and compute its color diversity score as the number of different nonzero colors it contains. Then extend a 2x2 green vertical trail downward from that pattern for exactly that many rows, starting one row beneath the original pattern.
